title: Shortest Path in Pauli Forest -- An Algorithm for Decomposing Pauli Exponentials to Quantum Circuits
authors: Lauri Vuorenkoski, Arianne Meijer-van de Griend
abstract: Decomposing Pauli exponentials efficiently to quantum circuits has been the subject of intense research in recent years. Pauli exponentials are an essential component of many different quantum algorithms. Due to the error-prone nature of current and near term quantum devices, it is crucial that quantum circuits are as compact as possible. Several different types of algorithms have been developed to decompose Pauli exponentials into as short circuits as possible. We propose a novel algorithm for architecture-aware synthesis of Pauli exponentials that also determines the initial qubit placement on the device. We call this the Shortest Path in Pauli Forest algorithm. The results show an improved CNOT count and runtime for both random Pauli exponentials and molecular ansätze.
summary: The paper introduces the 'Shortest Path in Pauli Forest' algorithm, which optimizes the synthesis of Pauli exponentials by treating decomposition as a path-finding problem. It achieves significant reductions in gate counts and execution time by being aware of the underlying quantum hardware architecture. This is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations more viable on noisy, near-term quantum devices.
main_problem: Efficiently decomposing Pauli exponentials into quantum circuits while minimizing CNOT count, CNOT depth, and runtime on NISQ-era hardware with specific connectivity constraints.
main_result: The proposed Shortest Path in Pauli Forest (SPPF) algorithm significantly reduces CNOT count (up to 71%) and runtime (up to 91%) compared to standard methods like Lexicographical Ordering and Qiskit/TKET for both random Pauli strings and molecular ansätze.
method: A heuristic algorithm that models decomposition as a shortest-path problem in a graph of Clifford tableaus, combined with an architecture-aware initial qubit mapping strategy.
model_or_system: The study focuses on NISQ-era quantum computing architectures with various topologies (Line, Grid, Guadalupe, and IBM Brisbane/Mumbai) and uses UCCSD molecular ansätze (e.g., H2, LiH, H2O) as application benchmarks.
key_observables: CNOT count, CNOT depth, and algorithm runtime.
important_parameters: Number of Pauli gadgets (g), number of qubits (n), and hardware connectivity/topology.
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes Pauli gadgets are mutually commuting (common in Trotterized Hamiltonians) and utilizes a specific Clifford gate set.
figures_summary: Figures demonstrate the decomposition of single gadgets on line topologies, illustrate the construction of connectivity trees on grid topologies, and provide comparative performance plots for CNOT count and runtime across different hardware architectures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Pauli exponential decomposition, reviews existing literature, proposes the SPPF algorithm and mapping strategy, presents performance evaluations on random and molecular systems across various topologies, and concludes with a discussion of results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Example of the decomposition of a single Pauli gadget restricted to a line topology. Distance of the path in this example is 4.
Fig. 2. Example of optimal mapping with line topology 0-1-2-3.
Fig. 3. Example of connectivity tree constructed on 16-grid topology.
Fig. 4. Example of gadget mapped to tree before pruning.
Fig. 5. Results on line topology with 16 qubits. Lexicographical ordering (LO) without mapping is the baseline.
